full_response:
We want the part of the Schwarzschild metric that involves t and r written in terms of the Kruskal null coordinates  

 U = –e^{-u/(4GM)}, V =  e^{v/(4GM)} ,

while leaving the angular piece r²dΩ² untouched.

1.  Start with the ordinary Schwarzschild line element  

 ds² = –(1 – 2GM/r) dt² + (1 – 2GM/r)^{-1} dr² + r² dΩ² . (1)

2.  Introduce the tortoise coordinate r_* defined by  

 dr_* = dr /(1 – 2GM/r)  ⇒ r_* = r + 2GM ln|r/2GM – 1| . (2)

   In (t,r_*) coordinates,

 ds² = –(1 – 2GM/r)(dt² – dr_*²) + r² dΩ² . (3)

3.  Define the Eddington–Finkelstein null coordinates  

 u = t – r_* , v = t + r_* . (4)

   Then

 dt = ½ (du + dv),  dr_* = ½ (dv – du),  
 dt² – dr_*² = du dv. (5)

   Hence the t–r part of the metric becomes  

 ds²_{t,r} = –(1 – 2GM/r) du dv. (6)

4.  Pass to the Kruskal null coordinates U, V:

 U = –e^{-u/(4GM)},  V = e^{v/(4GM)}. (7)

   Differentials:

 dU = –\dfrac{U}{4GM}\,du ,  dV = \dfrac{V}{4GM}\,dv  
 ⇒ du = –\dfrac{4GM}{U}\,dU , dv = \dfrac{4GM}{V}\,dV . (8)

   Therefore

 du\,dv = \left(-\dfrac{4GM}{U}\right)\!\left(\dfrac{4GM}{V}\right)dU dV
    = -\dfrac{16 G^{2} M^{2}}{UV}\,dU dV. (9)

5.  Substitute (9) into (6):

 ds²_{t,r} = -(1-2GM/r)\!\left(-\dfrac{16 G^{2} M^{2}}{UV}\right)dU dV
     = \dfrac{16 G^{2} M^{2}}{UV}(1-2GM/r)\,dU dV. (10)

6.  Relate UV to r.  From (7)

 UV = -e^{(v-u)/(4GM)} = -e^{2r_*/(4GM)} = -e^{\,r_*/(2GM)} (11)

 ⇒ \dfrac{1}{UV} = -e^{-r_*/(2GM)}. (12)

   Using (2): e^{-r_*/(2GM)} = e^{-r/(2GM)}\bigl(\frac{r}{2GM}-1\bigr)^{-1}
              = e^{-r/(2GM)}\dfrac{2GM}{r-2GM}. (13)

   Hence

 (1-2GM/r)\,e^{-r_*/(2GM)} = \dfrac{r-2GM}{r}\,\dfrac{2GM}{r-2GM}\,e^{-r/(2GM)}
               = \dfrac{2GM}{r}\,e^{-r/(2GM)}. (14)

7.  Insert (12) and (14) into (10):

 ds²_{t,r} = 16 G^{2} M^{2}\!\bigl[-e^{-r_*/(2GM)}\bigr]
       \left(\dfrac{2GM}{r}e^{-r/(2GM)}\right)dU dV
     = -32 G^{3} M^{3}\,\dfrac{e^{-r/(2GM)}}{r}\,dU dV. (15)

8.  Finally, reinstate the unchanged angular part:

 ds² = -\frac{32 G^{3} M^{3}}{r}\,e^{-r/(2GM)}\,dU dV + r^{2} d\Omega^{2}. (16)

This is exactly the standard Kruskal form of the Schwarzschild metric.

\boxed{\,ds^{2}= -\dfrac{32 G^{3} M^{3}}{r}\,e^{-\,r/(2GM)}\,dU\,dV + r^{2}\,d\Omega^{2}\,}

Confidence: 92%
